evidence: Anna explicitly requested this shift to reduce her logistics workload; Vrijen agreed absolutely
Event coordination responsibilities being handed off; meeting with Starbridge next week
I'm just using personal account, but we should centralize everything to Luma

Vrijen described himself as becoming a '1% or 0.1% power user' of products he embeds with—someone who dives so deep into a platform that he reaches functionality most users never discover. Yet he simultaneously identifies as 'non-technical'
Perhaps the most sophisticated users of any system are those who think in first principles about *how* the system should work, regardless of whether they can build it themselves

Vrijen has moved 12 cities across 33 years—bulk of that in first half of his life... The people who moved often may not be more flexible—they may be more practiced at recognizing when to stop trying to adapt and start trying to belong

Zain's entire thesis centers on what he calls 'human support systems'—infrastructure needed to navigate an AI-automated future... where do I want to spend? Like, what can I invest my time in that actually matters?
Both emphasized 'collaboration over competition'... Vrijen's work at Careerspan and Zo both aim to help humans become better self-advocates using AI tools

source_display: Small World Algorithm as Trust Mechanism
evidence: The Logan connection—Zain randomly connected with Vrijen's co-founder on TikTok... We optimize for efficiency, but trust is created by inefficiency. The 'small world' moments are valuable precisely *because* they weren't supposed to happen

Zain had randomly connected with Logan (Vrijen's co-founder) on TikTok after seeing her content about work and skills. Neither realized the shared connection until Vrijen mentioned it during their call

I have long been of the belief that, like, if you have substantive things to say, you don't need to rely on these like, cheap tactics... authenticity isn't a competitive strategy—it's a *defensive* one
One of my beliefs too is like, I mean for most part it's like collaboration over competition. I think there's just so much opportunity, especially for people who are looking at it from this angle
Vrijen recognizing alignment between Zain's interests and FOHE's mission, then offering concrete connection: 'check out fohi. I think obviously it's literally right up your alley'

Desktop optimization is ultimate like you can rationalize it as being highly relevant because it's your day to day space but it is also such a like complete like luxury... maybe all productivity is just identity performance

Careerspan isn't a replacement for Handshake or any recruiting platform—it's the 'missing layer' that makes their products defensible. This reframes the entire partnership narrative from competition to symbiosis
Position as 'missing layer' that makes their product defensible long-term. Frame as band-aid hedge that could rejuvenate their business line
Problem: access without signal is noise - 20M profiles with no way to identify who will succeed. Signal technology (Careerspan) completes the puzzle

Handshake has 1,600 universities but no way to capture behavioral signal at scale. Careerspan has the coaching infrastructure to capture signal but lacks distribution. The combination creates a data moat neither could build alone
Extended conversation about potential partnership with Handshake: Handshake's core recruiting business: ~$200M revenue, but only 6% growth in last year

The entire Careerspan thesis rests on the premise that behavioral signal (how people think) is more valuable than profile data (what they've done). This challenges the fundamental premise of LinkedIn and traditional resumes
Emphasizing the 3-person team as an asset (low acquisition overhead) turns a potential weakness into strength: we're not expensive to acquire, but we're strategically valuable
The real value isn't in helping someone get one job—it's in tracking their entire career trajectory and predicting future success. This creates a dataset that compounds in value over time
Strong rapport between Vrijen and Logan; Collaborative decision-making on strategy; No hesitation in challenging each other's ideas constructively
The repeated emphasis on leveraging founder club networks and warm introductions reveals an implicit strategy: careerspan is playing a relationship-heavy, founder-to-founder game

Careerspan's positioning around 'elite talent identification' represents a strategic pivot from general career coaching. This moves them upstream in the value chain—from helping anyone improve, to helping organizations identify top performers
